_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve is a graph plotting US Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years. A normal upward slope reflects higher yields on longer maturities, compensating for risk over time, while an inverted (downward sloping) curve historically precedes recessions by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.62% with the 10Y-2Y spread at 0.4 percentage points, indicating a modestly upward sloping curve. This contrasts with the 2022-2024 period when the curve was inverted, with the 2s10s spread at or below 0.0 points, marking the deepest inversion in the series history.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread, the difference between 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields, is a key recession indicator; inversion (spread below zero) has historically preceded US recessions. The curve was inverted during 2022-2024, signaling elevated recession risk, but has since re-steepened above zero to 0.4 points, signaling a normalization. The New York Fed recession probability currently stands at 31%, illustrating a moderate but not definitive risk of economic contraction. It is important to note that the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before recessions actually occur, which complicates timing interpretations based solely on the slope.
notes.now: The current modestly positive 2s10s spread and 4.62% 10-year yield suggest that, while recession risks remain relevant as indicated by the 31% NY Fed probability, the steepening yield curve reflects reduced immediate financial stress compared to the recent inversion period. The mixed signals imply economic conditions warrant vigilance but do not definitively forecast near-term recession onset.
